texts.label: text
texts.orig: New programs seeking to improve energy efficiency and reduce GHG emissions from the steelmaking industry are studying alternative uses for blast furnace gases such as  the  VALORCO  project  in  France.  One  promising  alternative  is  CO2  and  CO utilization in  which emissions are used for the production of valuable products, like industrial intermediates and fuels synthetized from CO and or CO2. A gas separation and  concentration  technology  is  necessary  to  recover  industry  emissions  and produce  gas  stream(s)  suitable  for  carbon  utilization  technologies.  This  paper describes the application of commercially available polymeric gas separation membranes to blast furnace gases. Data for two commercially available membranes, a H2 selective glassy polyimide membrane from UBE industries and a CO2 selective rubbery  composite  membrane  from  MTR  Inc.  along  with  a  process  economic modeling  have  been  used  for  the  calculation  of  product  composition  paths  and associated costs depending on product purity and recovery. Relatively low separation costs,  from  15  to  33  EUR/Ton  separated  CO2,  seem  promising  for  the  use  of membrane  technology  as  a  concentration  tuning  box  prior  to  carbon  utilization technologies, notably for mid  to high  CO2  purity requirements,  and  allow  to simultaneously achieve CO2 capture and CO recovery.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Industry  is  responsible  for  approximatively  one-third  of  global final  energy  use  and around  40%  of  total  energy-related  CO2  emissions.  Despite  continuous  efficiency increase in energy utilization, overall growing industrial production has led the CO2 emissions to rise [1]. This is particularly true for the iron and steel industry, where the production increase in the past two decades is closely related to the development of emergent  economies  such  as  China  and  India.  Global  steel  production  almost doubled between 2000 and 2014, going from 0.85 MTons/year to 1.67 MTons/year. GHG emissions depend on the production technology. Two production technologies account for most of the steel produced globally: Integrated steel mills based on the Blast  Furnace  -  Blast  Oxygen  Furnace  (BF-BOF),  and  the  Electric  Arc  Furnace based on recycled steel or scrap. Integrated steel mills dominate global production with  over  70%  of  crude  steel  worldwide  being  produced  by  this  process  and  the increase  in  steel  production  over  the  past  decades  was  mainly  achieved  by  an increase  of  production  by this  technology [2]. BF-BOF  technology  consumes between 13-14 GJ/ton of steel and can produce up to 1.9 Tons of CO2 per ton of steel  depending  on  energy  efficiency  and  the  percentage  of  carbonated  energy according to local conditions [3].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Blast furnace gas (BFG) emissions are the most important in volume and represent around 69% of the emitted CO2. BFG is mainly composed of 20-28% CO, 17-25% CO2, 50-55% N2 and 1-5% H2. It is typically burned for heating and power generation often  being  mixed  with  natural  gas,  coke  oven  gas  or  blast  oxygen furnace  gas  to increase its heating value.

It is typically burned for heating and power generation often  being  mixed  with  natural  gas,  coke  oven  gas  or  blast  oxygen furnace  gas  to increase its heating value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Energy and emissions concerns have led to research programs seeking to improve energy efficiency and reduce GHG emissions from the steelmaking industry. Recent works have studied the sequestration of CO2 from iron and steel off gases [4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]. New programs are studying alternative uses for blast furnace gases in order to reduce  emissions  such  as  the  VALORCO  project  in  France.  One  promising alternative is CO2 and CO utilization in which emissions are used for the production of valuable products, like industrial intermediates and fuels synthetized from CO and or CO2, either by chemical or biological transformations. This alternative would need a low energy, clean, safe separation technology (or technologies) capable of treating high volumes at economical rates.
texts.text: Energy and emissions concerns have led to research programs seeking to improve energy efficiency and reduce GHG emissions from the steelmaking industry. Recent works have studied the sequestration of CO2 from iron and steel off gases [4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]. New programs are studying alternative uses for blast furnace gases in order to reduce  emissions  such  as  the  VALORCO  project  in  France.  One  promising alternative is CO2 and CO utilization in which emissions are used for the production of valuable products, like industrial intermediates and fuels synthetized from CO and or CO2, either by chemical or biological transformations. This alternative would need a low energy, clean, safe separation technology (or technologies) capable of treating high volumes at economical rates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Technologies typically applied for CO2 and CO  separation share common characteristics in their operation, the main difference being the separation agent itself and  the  nature  of  its  interactions  with  the  gas  species  to  be  separated.  These separation processes include chemical absorption by amine systems in the case of CO2 retention, and by copper salt solutions for CO; physical absorption for CO2 by selexol or Rectisol processes; adsorption processes like pressure swing adsorption (PSA) or vacuum pressure swing adsorption (VPSA) over CO2 and/or CO selective adsorbents;  cryogenic  separation,  and  membrane  separation.  To  our  knowledge, only  two  recent  research  programs  have  proposed  separation  technologies  for  the specific case of the iron and steel industry and have carried out tests on pilot level installations, both within CO2 capture and storage scenarios. The european ULCOS program proposed a modification of the blast furnace, by which the blast furnace is fed by an oxygen stream instead of air thus eliminating the N2 in the off-gas. This offgas  goes  to  a  capture  unit  for  CO2  and  the  residual  gases,  containing  CO,  are recycled back to the BF as reducing gases. This is known as a Top Gas Recycled Blast Furnace (TGR-BF). For the capture step, a combination of PSA and cryogenics in  order  to  achieve  the  purity  necessary  for  CO2  storage  was  proposed [8]. The Japanese  COURSE  50  program  has  proposed  a  PSA  process  in  which  BFG undergoes two separate adsorption and desorption stages in series, the first one for CO2 recovery from the pretreated BFG and the second one for CO recovery from the off  gas of the first stage [9] .The project also studies the use of hydrogen for direct reduction of the iron in the blast furnace.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane  gas  separation  has  been  proposed  as  a  potential  technology  for  CO2 separation from flue gases with CO2 contents lower than those present in BFG, and it is  now  a  commercial  alternative  for  separation  of  CO2  in  the  natural  and  biogas industry as well as for CO and H2 purification in syngas applications. Part of this is associated to the  many advantages offered by this technology over more classical processes such as reduced environmental impact due to the absence of chemical systems, easy up and down scaling, ease of operation and control, no moving parts, no additional energy required besides the one required for gas flow, among others. However,  very  few  works  have  been  published  dealing  with  the  application  of membrane separation to steelmaking off-gases. This study addresses the question of the  application  of  commercially  available  gas  separation  membranes  to  BFG  gas separation for CO2 and CO utilization and aims to illustrate current opportunities and challenges.  The  overall  framework,  sketched  on Figure  2 ,  is  significantly  different from the post-combustion carbon capture and sequestration (CCS) situation, which has been investigated in numerous studies up to now [10, 11, 12, 13] ; the objective of the membrane separation box is indeed to offer, from the quaternary feed mixture (CO2, CO, N2, H2) a slight modification of the gas composition of the retentate and permeate streams, in order to facilitate one of the utilization possibilities. A very high CO2  recovery  and  purity  goal,  both  typically  ≥90%  for  CCS  applications,  is  not required.  Rather,  a  bulk  removal  of  one  species  (e.g.  CO2),  associated  to  a concentration increase of another (e.g. CO, H2), might be of interest in a  technical economic scenario.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More specifically, data for two representative and commercially available membranes, a glassy, H2 selective polyimide membrane from UBE industries and a rubbery, CO2 selective composite membrane from MTR Inc. have been used for the simulation of blast furnace gas separation in a membrane process by means of an inhouse developed gas permeation simulation tool. The effect of operating parameters (pressure  ratio,  membrane  surface,  stage  cut)  on  separation  performances  was studied  and  a  general  mapping  of  gas  compositions  attainable  with  the  available commercial membranes is presented and discussed under a gas utilization scenario. Energy and surface requirements are taken into consideration and separation costs are  expressed  per  ton  of  recovered  CO2.  Based  on  a  systematic  parametric sensitivity  analysis,  a  set  of  operating  conditions  has  been  identified  with  overall separation costs as low as 25 €/ton of CO2 for a two-stage separation with 95% CO2 recovery. Additionally a concentrated CO stream can be recovered on the reject (high pressure) side. The interest of this promising performances for carbon capture and utilization is discussed.

texts.orig: Despite  the  high  number  of  polymers  available  for  gas  separation,  only  a  limited number  has  made  it  to  commercial  production.  Most  common  polymers  used  in commercial gas separation modules include cellulose acetate, polysulfones, polycarbonates,  polyimides,  perfluoropolymers  and  polydimethylsiloxanes [16,17] . Permeability/permeance data of commercial membranes is often not available in the literature, and when it is, it is often limited to the gas couple for which the membrane is  mainly  commercialized.  This  makes  difficult  the  task  of  comparing  all  available membranes  for  a  given  application,  particularly  if  the  gas  to  be  treated  is  a multicomponent  mixture,  as  it  is  the  case  of  the  blast  furnace  gas.  For  a  gas utilization scenario based on CO2, a CO2 selective membrane is of interest in order to reach a stream with the highest level of purity and free of inert species such as N2 and Ar. Recently, a highly permeable membrane has been developed by MTR under the name Polaris ® .  Permeances for the gas species present in the BFG have been published in works dealing with CO2  capture in post-combustion scenarios. Permeance is reported to be ten times higher than commercial CO2 membranes with selectivity  among the highest reported [13] .  Furthermore, this membrane has been used  as  a reference of current  maximum  performance  offered  by  polymeric membranes  for  CO2  capture  applications [10, 18]. Hence,  this  membrane  is considered in  this  study  as  a  model  of  the  best  CO2  selective  polymer  membrane currently available in the market.

texts.orig: Additionally, given the H2 content in the BFG, a H2 permeable membrane could be of interest  in  order  to  recover  not  only  CO2  but  also  available  H2  which  is  also  a necessary  input  for  some  CO2 utilization  scenarios  such  as  catalytic  conversion. Permeance data for a highly selective  H2  membrane is available  for the  UBE  B-H membrane from  UBE industries [19] .  Permeance  and  selectivity  are  among  those currently  available for H2  separation from  process  streams [16] .  This  membrane is also  considered  here.  Pure  gas  permeance  data  for  both  MTR's  and  UBE's membranes are summarized in Table 2.

texts.orig: Process simulations were carried out in Aspen Plus® software, version 8.6. PengRobinson fluid package available in the software was used for energy calculations. Compressors  and  vacuum  pumps  are  considered  to  be  isentropic.  Isentropic efficiency  is  taken  as  0.8  and  mechanical  efficiency  is  taken  as  0.9.  Multi-stage operation with inter-stage cooling was calculated both for the feed compression and permeate vacuum  power  use under the following recommended  constraints: maximum  stage  outlet  gas  temperature  175°C,  and  equal  compression  ratios  for each stage. Resulting compression ratios per stage were ~3 or less. Either two or three  compression  stages  were  needed  depending  on  the  pressure  requirements. Shell and tube heat exchangers for gas cooling after each stage were calculated in countercurrent flow with a mean constant value for the heat transfer coefficient (U) of 105 W/m 2 K corresponding to the case of pressurized gases on the shell side and cooling water on the tube side. [ 21 ]. Cooling water inlet and outlet temperature were taken as 24°C and 34°C respectively. Gas outlet temperature was taken as 35°C.

The permeance is also used to describe the permeating properties of the membrane. It corresponds to the pressure normalized flux through the membrane and according to equation (1) it can be expressed as the ratio of permeability and membrane thickness / .  Permeance is often expressed in  gas  permeation  units  (GPU)  where  1  GPU  =  3.348x10 -10 [mol/(m 2 .s.Pa)]. texts.orig: First,  a  one  stage  separation  is  calculated  and  the  effect  of  operation  mode  (feed compression  vs. permeate  vacuum)  and  operation  parameters  (pressure  and membrane  surface)  over  the  separation  performances  and  cost  are  evaluated  for both membranes to assess attainable regions of CO2 and CO purity and recovery as well  as  economically  optimal  operating  regions.  Second,  a  two  stage  separation  is considered with the aim of further reducing N2 content in a CO2 concentrated stream in order to allow a potential utilization by catalytic pathways. Optimization of operation parameters under imposed separation constraints was undertaken for the two-stage design  with  the  objective  of  reaching  minimum  costs.  Aspen  Plus  built-in  SQP optimization method was used for this purpose. Objective function was given by the CO2 separation cost, equation (27) detailed in Table 3 and described in the following section.

texts.orig: Cost calculations were done by implementing the cost model presented in  Table 3 within  the  Aspen  Plus ® simulations  using  executable  Fortran  statements.  Cost parameters  chosen  are  presented  on Table  4.  Cost  model  and  cost  model parameters  used  here  are  based  on  those  reported  in  literature  published  in membrane process design for carbon capture applications [10, 11, 12, 13] .  Capital cost  includes  membrane area and membrane module skids, compression, vacuum pumps and heat exchangers. Membrane surface investment was estimated using a membrane cost of 40 EUR/m 2 . Membrane frame investment was calculated using the cost  equation  originally  proposed  by  Van  der  Sluijs  et  al. [25] and  modified  by Roussanaly et al . [10] to take into account the effect of operating pressure on the the projected  costs.  The  minimum  pressure  considered  for  the  membrane  frame  cost equation was 5 bar either for feed compression or permeate vacuum.  Compressor cost  was  calculated  supposing  a  centrifugal  compressor  and  using  the  regressed equation  from  an  estimating  chart  proposed  in  the  classical  literature  of  process economics [ 26 ]. A material factor of 2.5 for stainless steel is included. The equation predicts  costs  per  kW  in  the  range  of  common  fixed  values  used  in  most  studies (~1000 EUR/kW) for the compression range considered in this study.  The vacuum pump investment is calculated using a fixed cost of 1500 EUR/kW based of the value of  1000  USD/kW  reported  by  Zhai  et  al.  [ 18 ]  and  adjusting  for  construction  in stainless steel according to a factor of 2.0/1.3 related to carbon steel [ 26 ]. Shell and tube  heat  exchangers  cost  is  calculated  using  a  regressed  equation  from  [ 26 ]. Stainless steel material factor and pressure factor for 40 bar are included in the cost estimation.

texts.orig: concentration. Interestingly, the possibilities offered by the two membranes are rather close in terms of composition window (even though the corresponding stage cut and pressure ratios  are  significantly  different).  The  difficulty  to  change  the  CO/N2  ratio, which  reflects  the  similar  permeation  behavior  of  these  two  compounds  is  also highlighted.  It  is  obvious  that  the  information  reported  in  Figure  4  is  far  to  be exhaustive, but this provides an inventory of gas composition possibilities which is an absolute prerequisite as soon as a utilization process is considered (e.g. what is the minimum CO2 content if a methanation process is aimed? What is the minimum CO content for  a  methanol  synthesis  application?).  A  one  stage  membrane  separation logically produces a retentate stream, rich in slow permeating gases (CO, N2), and a permeate  stream,  rich  in  fast  permeating  gases  (CO2,  H2).  In  a  feed  compression operation, because CO  conversion processes usually require high pressure conditions, the possibility to simultaneously provide a bulk removal for CO2 and the production  of  a  concentrated  pressurized  CO  stream  at  the  retentate  is  of  major interest.  This  situation  largely  differs  from  the  post-combustion  capture  from  flue gases, where CO2 removal generates a N2 rich stream with non-economic interest. If the key is rather an enrichment of CO2 in the permeate stream at low cost, and a low pressure of CO is not an issue, permeate vacuum operation will probably be more cost-effective  than  feed  compression,  as  it  has  been  shown  for  flue  gas  in  CCS applications [ 18 ].

texts.orig: We present here the performance of the separation in terms of product purity and recovery. Based on the identified composition paths, CO2 is defined as the product in the permeate stream, and CO is the product on the retentate stream. The CO2 and CO purity / recovery trade-off curves are shown on Figure 5. The performances of the two membranes are close in terms of CO purity / recovery trade-off, figures c) and d); for both cases a maximum CO purity in the retentate of ~30% can be achieved with recovery rates around 80%. Higher recoveries can be obtained at higher stage cuts by sacrificing CO purity. A significant difference is however observed for permeate CO2, where the superiority of the MTR Polaris membrane is noticeable, up to a 90% purity  can  be  reached with a 80% recovery vs. ~62% purity for the same recovery with  the  UBE  membrane. Higher feed pressure (and thus higher pressure ratio for constant permeate pressure) improves separation performance, however the effect becomes  less  marked  as  pressure  increases.  Higher  feed  pressure  means  higher compression  needs  and  so  more  energy  requirements.  The  overall  interplay  of variables  has  to  include  a  cost  analysis  in  order  to  frame  the  separation  within  an economical range.

texts.orig: Based  on  the  cost  model  presented  in  Table  3,  costs  in  terms  of  specific  CO2 separation cost are presented in Figure 6a and 6b for the UBE and MTR membrane respectively for a feed compression operation. Each cost curve for a constant feed pressure  goes  through  a  minimum  at  a  given  product  purity.  Trying  to  produce  a purer  product  beyond  this  minimum  results  in  an  exponential  increase  of  costs.  In such a case, it  is  more  economical  to  increase  feed  pressure  (i.e.  pressure  ratio). This is explained by the separation tradeoff presented in Figure 5. The minimum in the  cost  curves  correlates  to  the  inflexion  point  of  the  tradeoff  curves,  for  which  a higher purity is reached at the expense of a pronounced lost in recovery. In such a region,  the  same  purity  can  be  obtained  with  a  higher  recovery  by  increasing  the pressure ratio. The higher product recovery compensates for the higher cost related to compression energy and capital cost, and the overall separation cost is lower in terms of EUR/ton CO2. For CO2 purities below the minimum, it is more economical to operate at lower pressures as long as there is no much difference in recovery. This is exemplified by the cost curve for the UBE membrane at a feed pressure of 5 bar. For purities below 40% CO2, separation cost is actually lower at higher pressure. This is also explained by the differences in product recovery with pressure ratio.
texts.text: Based  on  the  cost  model  presented  in  Table  3,  costs  in  terms  of  specific  CO2 separation cost are presented in Figure 6a and 6b for the UBE and MTR membrane respectively for a feed compression operation. Each cost curve for a constant feed pressure  goes  through  a  minimum  at  a  given  product  purity.  Trying  to  produce  a purer  product  beyond  this  minimum  results  in  an  exponential  increase  of  costs.  In such a case, it  is  more  economical  to  increase  feed  pressure  (i.e.  pressure  ratio). This is explained by the separation tradeoff presented in Figure 5. The minimum in the  cost  curves  correlates  to  the  inflexion  point  of  the  tradeoff  curves,  for  which  a higher purity is reached at the expense of a pronounced lost in recovery. In such a region,  the  same  purity  can  be  obtained  with  a  higher  recovery  by  increasing  the pressure ratio. The higher product recovery compensates for the higher cost related to compression energy and capital cost, and the overall separation cost is lower in terms of EUR/ton CO2. For CO2 purities below the minimum, it is more economical to operate at lower pressures as long as there is no much difference in recovery. This is exemplified by the cost curve for the UBE membrane at a feed pressure of 5 bar. For purities below 40% CO2, separation cost is actually lower at higher pressure. This is also explained by the differences in product recovery with pressure ratio.

texts.orig: The difference in cost between the two membranes is more evident when compared for  the  same  operation  conditions  as  in  Figure  7.  Under  the  same  operation conditions, i.e. pressure ratio and membrane area, the MTR membrane offers both a better  CO2  purity  and  recovery  and  hence  lower  CO2  separation  cost.  For  high recovery separations at constant pressure ratio, high membrane area is needed, and as area increases, separation cost differences  become  smaller.  Cost  curves eventually become identical for very high recoveries with uninteresting product purity for very high membrane surface.

texts.orig: So far, pressure ratio has been varied by feed compression only. It is also possible however to create the driving force by generating a vacuum on the permeate side, either by itself only or in combination with some feed compression to create a mixed operation. These three approaches are compared on Figure 8 for the same pressure ratio. For increasing CO2 recoveries as high as ~95%, CO2 separation cost is always lower for the vacuum operation than for the compression of the feed. Mixed operation costs are located in between. For higher recoveries the cost curves come closer and overlap in the 20 -25 EUR/ton CO2 zone. For recoveries gradually approaching 99%, the  cost  curve  for  vacuum  operation  starts  increasing  exponentially,  and  the  lower separation  costs  are  offered  first  by  the  mixed  operation  and  then  by  the  feed compression.

texts.orig: These cost trends reflect  the  underlying  relationship  between membrane  area  and power  use  and  their  relative  importance  on  cost  for  each  operation  mode.  Feed compression  requires  less  membrane  surface  than  the  vacuum  operation  at  the same pressure ratio. On the other hand, vacuum operation requires only a fraction of the  energy  needed  for  feed  compression.  A  cost  breakdown  for  each  operation mode, as presented in Figure 9, illustrates this tradeoff. The three operation modes are  compared  in  order  to  produce  a  stream  with  ~70%  CO2  content  at  the  lowest separation cost in EUR/ton CO2. CO2 recovery is around 85% for this purity and a pressure ratio of 10.

Operational expenditures are in all  cases  mainly  attributed  to  energy  cost,  however  energy  cost  for  the  vacuum operation  is  only  roughly  30%  of  the  cost  for  the  compression  case.  For  the considered scenario, both the CAPEX and OPEX contribute to the lower costs of the vacuum operation compared to the feed compression case, yet the savings in power consumption through OPEX are more pronounced.

texts.orig: The  applied  economic  model  is  based  on  cost  parameters  typically  used  in  CO2 capture research programs. For a particular project these parameters can vary both geographically and chronologically as well as be significantly different under specific project  circumstances.  It  is  of  interest  to  evaluate  the  effect  of  variations  of  these parameters on the final separation cost. Figure 10 presents a sensitivity analysis of the specific separation cost for a process looking to achieve a product stream with 70% CO2 content and a CO2 recovery of 85%. Results are given for both a permeate vacuum and a feed compression operation. As predicted from the cost breakdown, the separation cost under vacuum is most sensitive to membrane life and membrane cost  which  cause  the  largest  variations.  Energy  cost  impact  is  similar  to  the membrane  cost  and  reflects  the  importance  of  the  energy  expenses,  even  in  the vacuum operation case. Less pronounced variations are attributed to the capital cost of the vacuum pump and heat exchangers as well as cooling water and O&M cost factors. Membrane frame cost has little impact on separation cost, explained by its little  share  of  capital  costs.  For  the  feed  compression  case,  it  is the electricity  cost that is responsible for the largest variations in cost. Energy cost, and hence power needs,  deserve  special  care  during  cost  calculations,  especially  for  compression scenarios.  Equipment  and  operation  cost  parameters  such  as  compressor  cost, cooling  water  cost,  O&M  cost,  and  heat  exchanger  cost  have  a  more  important impact  on  separation cost  than  membrane parameters such as membrane lifetime and  membrane  cost.  Membrane  frame  has  again  little  impact  on  separation  cost. These results illustrate the cost  parameters that deserve particular attention during cost calculation of membrane separation designs and provide insight for designers of cost-effective  gas  membrane  separations.  So  far,  we  have  discussed  one-stage separation possibilities within a general utilization perspective, available composition paths and associated costs depending on product gas purity and recovery has been presented. Next section deals with a specific carbon utilization alternative, catalytic transformation,  and  explores  the  separation  capabilities  of  a  two  stage  membrane design, and its ability to produce a gas with higher purity and higher recovery.

texts.orig: 3.2 Two  stage  separation  for  CO2  enrichment  and  utilization  by  catalytic transformation
texts.text: 3.2 Two  stage  separation  for  CO2  enrichment  and  utilization  by  catalytic transformation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most interesting utilization pathways for CO and CO2 is the production of valuable  chemical  products  such  as  methanol,  urea,  and  methane  among  others species by means of catalytic conversion processes [ 27,  28,  29,  30 ].  Both  product streams  from  the  BFG  membrane  separation,  a  CO-rich  retentate  and  a  CO2-rich permeate could be potential candidates to be used as feedstock for such transformation processes. Table 5 lists the main reactions that take place during the production of chemicals from CO and CO2.
texts.text: One of the most interesting utilization pathways for CO and CO2 is the production of valuable  chemical  products  such  as  methanol,  urea,  and  methane  among  others species by means of catalytic conversion processes [ 27,  28,  29,  30 ].  Both  product streams  from  the  BFG  membrane  separation,  a  CO-rich  retentate  and  a  CO2-rich permeate could be potential candidates to be used as feedstock for such transformation processes. Table 5 lists the main reactions that take place during the production of chemicals from CO and CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One  of  the  limitations  of  feed  streams  for  catalytic  processes  is  the  presence  of impurities along with the reactants. Depending on the nature of the impurities these can be toxic for the catalysts, such as oxygen in the case of CO2 reduction catalysts, or  they  can  be  inert  components  that  reduce  the  concentration/partial  pressure  of reactants resulting in increase of reaction volumes and loss of reactants through inert purge streams. The main inert component present in both product streams from a one stage membrane separation is N2, especially in the CO-rich retentate for which N2 content is between 55 to 75%. As explained before, dense polymeric membranes are  not  suited  for  CO-N2  separations,  and  so  another  technology  must  be  used  if further CO enrichment is needed.

texts.orig: On the other hand, N2 content in the permeate side can reach values under 10% but it does so at very low stage cuts/CO2 recoveries. However, permeate N2 content can be  further  reduced  while  keeping  high  recoveries  by  a  multistage  membrane separation  as  it  has  been  proposed  for  CO2 capture  scenarios  [ 10,  12 ].  Such  a process  is  presented  in  Figure  11,  considering  a  two-stage  permeate  enricher configuration within the utilization perspective discussed above. Driving force choice depends on the utilization technologies to be coupled with the membrane separation. Feed compression would allow the production of a pressurized CO rich stream, which could  be  of  interest  depending  on  the  utilization  process  conditions  and  if  optional separation steps are needed and could take advantage of the high pressure. Another possibility  is  the  compression of the  product stream after separation instead of the feed  stream,  which  would  lead  to  lower  energy  requirements  because  a  smaller volume is compressed, and product pressure could be adjusted independently of the membrane separation step. Driving force is created here by exerting a vacuum on the permeate  side.  As  showed  in  Figure  8,  for  moderate  CO2  recoveries,  vacuum operation offers the lowest CO2 separation costs.

texts.orig: The two-stage permeate enricher allows the reduction of N2 content under 5% and down to 1% for the operation range presented in Figure 12. For a constant and equal pressure ratio in each stage, further increasing by two the membrane area of the first stage  has  little  effect  over  the  product  N2  content.  The  effect  of  this  increase  is however  not  negligible  over  CO2  recovery  and  separation  cost,  as  it  is  illustrated Figure  13.  Cost  associated  to  the  additional  membrane  area  in  the  first  stage  is clearly  superior  independently  of  the  membrane  area  of  the  second  stage.  Overall CO2 recovery however, can actually be lower for an increase in membrane area of the first stage if the second stage area is set at a low value, around 2x10 5 m 2 for the case  presented.  Furthermore,  the  minimum  in  the  cost  curves  are  related  to  the inflexion points seen in the CO2 recovery curves that are present for the same values of  membrane  area.  These  results  illustrate  the  increasing  complexity  of  parameter interaction for multistage architectures, even for a two stage separation.

texts.orig: Optimization of process variables is needed in order to identify operation zones with the minimum cost that fulfill separation requirements given by the selected utilization pathway. One of the advantages of the developed tool is that it allows the use of the built-in optimization tools within the process software. Membrane surface for the two stages has been optimized with the objective of minimizing CO2 separation cost of the two stage enricher. As before, pressure ratio is kept constant and equal for both stages to reduce the complexity of the analysis. The resulting optimal separation cost for varying product constraints is presented in Table 6. A maximum N2 content of 5% was imposed as constraint for increasing recovery ratios of CO2 from 90 to 99% for the first three cases. A more restrictive N2 content of 1% was imposed for the fourth case along with a 90% CO2 recovery. CO2 recoveries higher than 91% for the 1% N2 content constraint were not feasible under the simulated conditions. The increase in the CO2 recovery constraint has a notable effect on the optimal separation cost and product  composition.  For  the  case  with  99%  recovery,  N2  content  reaches  the  5% constraint, and higher recoveries would therefore not respect the 5% constraint. The 99% CO2 case has the highest cost with around 33.2 EUR/Ton CO2. Comparison between  the  first  and  fourth  cases  shows  that  a  reduction  of  N2  content  can  be reached  at  90%  CO2  recovery  with  a  slight  increase  of  costs  resulting  from  an increase in total membrane area. This could be interesting for  utilization paths that are very sensitive to the presence of inert species on the feed. CO2 purity is around 91% for both cases at 90% recovery. It lowers to 87% and 81% for the cases at 95% and 99% recovery respectively.

texts.orig: This study intended to address the problem of blast furnace flue gas separation for carbon utilization purposes. The possibilities and limitations of two different (i.e. one glassy and one rubbery) commercially available membranes have been investigated through a parametric and technical-economic simulation. The situation largely differs from post-combustion carbon capture framework in that a complex feed mixture has to be treated (CO2, CO, N2, H2) and a CO rich stream, offering an added value, can be generated simultaneously to CO2 removal. The different composition ranges of a single stage and a two stage membrane process and the associated costs have been evaluated.
texts.text: This study intended to address the problem of blast furnace flue gas separation for carbon utilization purposes. The possibilities and limitations of two different (i.e. one glassy and one rubbery) commercially available membranes have been investigated through a parametric and technical-economic simulation. The situation largely differs from post-combustion carbon capture framework in that a complex feed mixture has to be treated (CO2, CO, N2, H2) and a CO rich stream, offering an added value, can be generated simultaneously to CO2 removal. The different composition ranges of a single stage and a two stage membrane process and the associated costs have been evaluated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The difficulty to achieve CO/N2 separation by membrane processes (but also by any process, be it cryogenics, absorption or adsorption) is a major limitation for pushing CO purity performances. Despite relatively low purity, high partial pressure CO can be produced in the retentate by membrane separation under feed compression. Such operation would produce two valuable streams, a high partial pressure CO product and a high purity CO2 product which could be both feed streams for carbon utilization processes. If CO2 separation cost is the main concern and/or if the CO stream must undergo  further  purification  and  thus  a  high  partial  pressure  is  not  an  asset,  a vacuum operation allows a reduction of costs compared to the feed compression. A two stage membrane process allows permeate CO2 recoveries of up to 99% while keeping  inert  N2  content  below  5%  at  costs  ranging  from  23  to  33  EUR/Ton  CO2. Lower inert contents can be achieved for lower recoveries at comparable separation costs.  This  stream  comes  with  H2  content  up  to  5%  which  can  be  interesting  for catalytic carbon utilization using H2 as reactant.
texts.text: The difficulty to achieve CO/N2 separation by membrane processes (but also by any process, be it cryogenics, absorption or adsorption) is a major limitation for pushing CO purity performances. Despite relatively low purity, high partial pressure CO can be produced in the retentate by membrane separation under feed compression. Such operation would produce two valuable streams, a high partial pressure CO product and a high purity CO2 product which could be both feed streams for carbon utilization processes. If CO2 separation cost is the main concern and/or if the CO stream must undergo  further  purification  and  thus  a  high  partial  pressure  is  not  an  asset,  a vacuum operation allows a reduction of costs compared to the feed compression. A two stage membrane process allows permeate CO2 recoveries of up to 99% while keeping  inert  N2  content  below  5%  at  costs  ranging  from  23  to  33  EUR/Ton  CO2. Lower inert contents can be achieved for lower recoveries at comparable separation costs.  This  stream  comes  with  H2  content  up  to  5%  which  can  be  interesting  for catalytic carbon utilization using H2 as reactant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given the diversity and complexity of carbon dioxide capture and use (sketched on Figure 2), large efforts are required in order to better estimate the best place and role of  membranes  in  the  steel  manufacturing  industry.  The  optimal  set  of  conditions which minimizes the overall cost of a complete separation / conversion process, the role of minor species and impurities for each utilization option remain important open questions.  Laboratory  and  field  tests  on  real  flue  gases,  in  order  to  determine  the impact of dust particles, humidity and the stability of membrane module performances is also necessary and is the focus of upcoming work in our team. The preliminary  conclusions  offered  by  this  exploratory  study  suggest  however  that  a concentration  tuning  box  based  on  membranes  and  applied  on  blast  furnace  flue gases could be of interest in order to simultaneously achieve CO2 capture and CO recovery.
